title: Terminal AI — Copilot CLI: The Who, the How, and the Why

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you will install, configure, and master GitHub Copilot CLI directly in your terminal.

Your challenge: complete a series of real-world terminal tasks using only Copilot CLI. By the end of this session, you will have generated commands, explained unfamiliar syntax, and built a set of productivity aliases; all without searching the web for a single command.

Follow the steps in this panel as you advance through the slides.

steps.title: Verify GitHub CLI
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and check that the GitHub CLI is installed and up to date:

gh --version

You need version 2.40 or later. If the command is not found, install it from cli.github.com before proceeding.

steps.title: Confirm Authentication
steps.instruction:
Verify that you are authenticated with GitHub:

gh auth status

You should see your username and the scopes granted. If you are not logged in, run gh auth login and follow the prompts.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Install the Copilot Extension
steps.instruction:
Install the Copilot CLI extension:

gh extension install github/gh-copilot

If you already have it installed, you may see a message saying so. That is fine.

steps.title: Verify Copilot CLI
steps.instruction:
Confirm the installation succeeded:

gh copilot --version

You should see a version number. If you want the latest features, run:

gh extension upgrade gh-copilot
steps.tip: Copilot CLI is lightweight. It does not install background services or modify your shell profile.

steps.title: Check Extension Logs
steps.instruction:
Open the Output panel and select GitHub Copilot from the dropdown. Look for any warnings or errors from the Copilot CLI extension.

This is your go-to troubleshooting tool when Copilot commands behave unexpectedly.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`
• Output Panel: Ctrl+Shift+U

steps.title: Your First Suggestion
steps.instruction:
Time to try gh copilot suggest. Ask Copilot to generate a command for you:

gh copilot suggest "list all files in the current directory sorted by size"

Copilot will return a shell command. Review it, then choose Execute, Copy, or Revise.

steps.title: Specify a Target Type
steps.instruction:
Now try a git-specific suggestion:

gh copilot suggest -t git "show the last 5 commits with file names"

The -t git flag restricts suggestions to Git commands. Try -t gh as well to see GitHub CLI suggestions.

steps.title: Challenge: Revise a Suggestion
steps.instruction:
Run a suggest command, but this time choose Revise when the result appears. Add a constraint to your original request and see how Copilot refines the output.

Hint: start broad (e.g., "compress this folder") and then refine with an exclusion or format requirement.
steps.tip: The Revise option preserves context from your previous request, so each refinement builds on the last.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Explain a Complex Command
steps.instruction:
Pass a command you want to understand to gh copilot explain:

gh copilot explain "find . -name '*.ts' -exec grep -l 'TODO' {} +"

Read the breakdown. Copilot explains each flag, argument, and operator individually.

steps.title: Challenge: Explain Something Unfamiliar
steps.instruction:
Think of a command you have seen but never fully understood. It could be a git, docker, kubectl, or any shell command. Pass it to gh copilot explain and see if the explanation matches your expectations.

If you need inspiration, try:

gh copilot explain "git rebase -i --autosquash HEAD~5"

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Interactive Mode
steps.instruction:
Run gh copilot suggest with no arguments:

gh copilot suggest

Copilot will walk you through a guided flow: choose a target type, then enter your description. This is ideal for exploration.

steps.title: One-Shot Mode
steps.instruction:
Now run the same request in one-shot mode by passing your description inline:

gh copilot suggest "check which process is using port 3000"

Compare the experience. One-shot is faster when you know exactly what you need.
steps.tip: Most experienced users start with interactive mode when learning a new tool, then graduate to one-shot mode.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Shell Awareness Challenge
steps.instruction:
Copilot detects your active shell and tailors suggestions accordingly. Try this request in your current terminal:

gh copilot suggest -t shell "get disk usage for the current drive"

If you are in PowerShell, you should get a PowerShell cmdlet. If you are in bash, you should get a Unix command. Notice how the tool choice changes, not just the syntax.

steps.title: Target Type Comparison
steps.instruction:
Run the same natural-language request with different target types and compare the results:

• gh copilot suggest -t shell "list my open pull requests"
• gh copilot suggest -t gh "list my open pull requests"

The -t shell version may produce a curl command; the -t gh version will produce a clean gh pr list command. Choosing the right target type matters.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: CLI for the Command, Chat for the Code
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and use Copilot CLI to generate a Docker command:

gh copilot suggest "run a node 20 container with the current directory mounted and port 3000 exposed"

Now open Copilot Chat and ask it to scaffold the application code that would run inside that container. Notice how each tool handles what it does best.

steps.title: Know Your Boundaries
steps.instruction:
Try asking Copilot CLI something that requires file context:

gh copilot suggest "refactor the server module to use async/await"

The result will be generic because CLI cannot see your files. This is a task for Copilot Chat, not CLI. Recognizing when to switch tools is a key skill.
steps.tip: Rule of thumb: if the task is about running a command, use CLI. If the task is about writing or understanding code, use Chat.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Git Challenge
steps.instruction:
Use gh copilot suggest -t git to solve these Git tasks. Do not look up the answers; let CLI generate the commands for you:

• Undo your last commit but keep the changes staged
• Show all files changed in the last 7 days
• Find which commit introduced a specific string in the codebase

Try at least two of these. Execute the ones that are safe, and copy the rest for reference.

steps.title: Explain Before You Execute
steps.instruction:
Pick the most complex command Copilot suggested in the previous step. Copy it and pass it to gh copilot explain.

This suggest-then-explain workflow is a safe habit: you generate the command, understand it, and then decide whether to run it.
steps.tip: Always review commands involving force-push, reset, or delete before executing them.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: File System Challenge
steps.instruction:
Use Copilot CLI to tackle these file-system tasks without searching the web:

• Find all TypeScript files modified in the last 24 hours
• Recursively search for TODO comments, excluding node_modules
• Count lines of code by file extension in the current directory

Use gh copilot suggest for each one. Compare the commands Copilot generates with what you would have written manually.

steps.title: System Detective
steps.instruction:
Try a system-administration query:

gh copilot suggest "check which process is using port 8080"

If the answer uses a tool you haven't seen before, pass it to gh copilot explain to learn what it does.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Discover GitHub CLI Commands
steps.instruction:
Use the -t gh target type to discover GitHub CLI commands you may not have known existed:

gh copilot suggest -t gh "list all failed workflow runs in the last week"

Try a few more: listing issues, creating releases, or checking Codespaces. The -t gh flag is like having a guided tour of the GitHub CLI.

steps.title: Challenge: GitHub Workflow Automation
steps.instruction:
Come up with a real GitHub task you do manually (e.g., labeling issues, listing PRs by reviewer, checking Actions minutes). Use gh copilot suggest -t gh to find the command for it.

If the suggestion includes flags you do not recognize, explain it before running it.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Create Your Aliases
steps.instruction:
Set up shortcut aliases so Copilot CLI is always at your fingertips. In your terminal, create temporary aliases for this session:

PowerShell:
function cs { gh copilot suggest @args }
function ce { gh copilot explain @args }

Bash/Zsh:
alias cs='gh copilot suggest'
alias ce='gh copilot explain'

Test them: cs "show current git branch"

steps.title: Test Your Aliases
steps.instruction:
Verify your aliases work by running a few quick commands:

cs "list all running docker containers"
ce "docker ps --format 'table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}'"

If the aliases work, you have reduced the friction to near zero.
steps.tip: To make aliases permanent, add them to your shell profile: $PROFILE for PowerShell, ~/.bashrc for bash, or ~/.zshrc for zsh.

steps.title: Find Your Shell Profile
steps.instruction:
Open the Explorer and browse to your shell profile file. For PowerShell, the profile is at $PROFILE (usually Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1). For bash/zsh, look for .bashrc or .zshrc in your home directory.

This is where you would add aliases permanently. Review the file to see if you already have customizations you should preserve.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E

steps.title: Challenge: Build a Specialized Function
steps.instruction:
Create a shell function that combines a target type with your alias. For example, a gitsuggest function that always passes -t git:

PowerShell:
function gitsuggest { gh copilot suggest -t git @args }

Bash:
gitsuggest() { gh copilot suggest -t git "$*"; }

Test it with a git task of your choice.

steps.title: Dotfiles Challenge
steps.instruction:
Think about how you would add these aliases and functions to your dotfiles repository so they follow you across machines. Open a new file and draft your additions.

Hint: for PowerShell, the profile lives at $PROFILE. For bash/zsh, it is ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc. You can verify the path with:

echo $PROFILE (PowerShell) or echo $HOME/.bashrc (bash)

steps.title: Configure Your Default Shell
steps.instruction:
Open the Command Palette and search for Terminal: Select Default Profile. This lets you choose which shell Copilot CLI runs in by default.

Since Copilot CLI adapts its suggestions to your active shell, your default profile choice affects every suggestion you receive.

steps.title: Check Terminal Settings
steps.instruction:
Open Settings and search for terminal.integrated. Review the shell integration settings — these affect how VS Code interacts with your terminal and can improve the Copilot CLI experience.

Key settings to check:
• terminal.integrated.defaultProfile — your default shell
• terminal.integrated.shellIntegration.enabled — enables rich shell features
• terminal.integrated.suggest.enabled — terminal suggestions

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`
• Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P
• Settings: Ctrl+,

steps.title: Precision Challenge
steps.instruction:
Test the impact of prompt specificity. Run two versions of the same request:

• Vague: cs "list files"
• Specific: cs "list all .ts files modified in the last 7 days sorted by size descending"

Compare the results. The specific request should produce a significantly more useful command.

steps.title: Suggest-Then-Explain Loop
steps.instruction:
Run a suggest command for something you are not sure about. Copy the resulting command and pipe it into ce (your explain alias):

• Step 1: cs "find all git merge conflicts in the current repo"
• Step 2: Copy the result
• Step 3: ce "<paste the command here>"

This loop is the safest way to use Copilot CLI: generate, understand, then execute.
steps.tip: Combine explain with suggest as a habit. You learn new command-line patterns every time.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Pitfall Awareness
steps.instruction:
Before you finish, test your judgment. Use gh copilot suggest for a potentially destructive operation:

cs "remove all untracked files and directories from this git repo"

When the command appears, do not execute it immediately. Read it carefully. Does it include flags like -f or -d? Pass it to ce to understand each flag before deciding whether to run it.

This is the most important habit: always review before executing destructive commands.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Challenge complete! You have finished Copilot CLI: The Who, the How, and the Why.

You now know how to:
• Install and configure Copilot CLI with a single command
• Generate shell, git, and GitHub CLI commands with suggest
• Break down unfamiliar commands with explain
• Choose between interactive and one-shot modes
• Combine CLI and Chat for full coverage
• Build aliases and shell functions for near-zero friction
• Review commands safely before executing

Next courses: Enterprise-Grade Marmalade for governance, or Agent Potato: License to Chat for Copilot agent mode.
